assignment_description:
Introduction
⍞ Materials: Who Runs This Country, Anyway?

➜ INTRO
How would you answer the question that is in the title of the book you will begin reading today? Who is in charge in Canada? How does our country make decisions? How does a law get made? What happens during an election? In this course, you will learn about the electoral process, the different aspects of parliamentary government, the Canadian legal system, and the concept of citizenship.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.1-6 "All week our classmates" - "in a moment." (Audiobook chapters 1-2)

➜ NOTEBOOK
The Citizenship Notebook is an excellent place to keep track of definitions and to copy in diagrams. If any of these terms are new to you, you may choose to include definitions in your notebook: democracy, municipal, federal, parliament, legislature, legislative, executive, judicial. As you read through the book this year, look for other terms that are new to you. The diagram on page 6 would also be a good one to copy.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
If you are using the audio recording of the book, the readings will correspond with the audiobook chapters (with timestamps, if necessary), and the recordings clearly denote the corresponding page numbers.
Students can be encouraged to take notes as they listen to the audio recording.

Introduction to Three Levels of Government

➜ INTRO
This term, you will learn more about the three levels of government for your own area. This will require independent online research, so be sure to consult with your parent or teacher.

➜ REFLECT
Before looking anything up, can you name the three levels of government? Do you remember the titles of the heads of each level, and do you know the names of the individuals who fill these roles in your local area?

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Levels of Government (Video)

➜ READ
∞ Article Link: Levels of Government

➜ NOTEBOOK
Divide a blank page into three columns, and label each with the name of each level of government. Using the links below, enter in as much
information as you can regarding the structure and responsibilities of each.
➜ EXPLORE
Test your knowledge with the following activities. If you run out of time today, you can test your knowledge during the next lesson.
∞ Link: Activity: Levels of Government
∞ Link: Around Town Game: Levels of Government

Federal Government

➜ INTRO
Canada is divided into electoral districts, each one represented by a Member of Parliament (MP). These districts are also called ridings or
constituencies. Because these areas are determined by population rather than area, some can be geographically very large while others are smaller.
Today, you are going to do some research about your local federal electoral district. By using the resources below, and through additional
research with help from your parent or teacher, see if you can answer the following questions:
How many electoral districts are there in Canada?
What is the name of your local electoral district?
Can you find a map of it?
Who is your MP?
What party do they belong to?
Who were the other candidates in the most recent election?
What percentage of the vote did each candidate receive?
Can you find out more information about your MP from their own website?

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Who are parliamentarians?
∞ Link: Home Page for the 2025 Federal Election

➜ EXPLORE
∞ Link: Activity: Parliament and Elections

➜ READ & NARRATE
Find a current events article about the federal government. Narrate your chosen article either orally or written.

Provincial Government

➜ INTRO
Each province and territory has a legislative assembly, forming the provincial level of government. Each province and territory is divided into electoral districts with a representative to the legislative assembly from each district. The boundaries of your provincial electoral district may differ from the federal one. The provincial representative have difference titles in different provinces/territories (e.g. Member of Provincial Parliament (MPP) in Ontario, Members of the National Assembly (MNA) in Quebec, Members of the House of Assembly (MHA) in Newfoundland and Labrador, and Members of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) in most other places). The leader of the party with the most seats becomes the premier.
Each province also has a Lieutenant Governor who, like the Governor General, is the representative of the King of Canada for each province and territory.
Today, you are going to do some research about your local provincial electoral district. By using the resources below, and through additional research with help from your parent or teacher, see if you can answer the following questions:
Who is the premier of your province/territory and what is their political party?
What is the name of your local provincial electoral district?
Can you find a map of it?
Who is your representative, and what title do they have in your province/territory?
What party do they belong to?
When was the most recent election?
Who were the other candidates in the most recent election?
What percentage of the vote did each candidate receive?
Can you find out more information about your representative from their own website?
Who is your Lieutenant Governor and how long have they served in this role?

➜ VIEW
Find the Elections website for your home province or territory (use search terms "election" and name of province/territory)

➜ READ & NARRATE
Find a current events article about your provincial or territorial government. Narrate your chosen article either orally or written.

Local Government

➜ INTRO
Today, you will look at local government. Local governments are a paradox, because we most often forget that they exist, while they often
have the most direct impact on our daily lives.
The head the of the municipal government is usually called a mayor, but may have a different title in some places. Your municipality might be the same as your city, or, for smaller towns and rural areas, the municipality might be made up of several communities or a wider area. With a the help of a parent or teacher look up the website for your municipality and try to answer the following questions:
What is the name of your municipality?
Can you find a map of it?
Who is the mayor (or other title) of your municipality?
What other representatives make up your local government and what are their titles?
When was the most recent election?
Who were the other candidates in the most recent election?
What percentage of the vote did each candidate receive?
Can you find out more information about your mayor from their own website?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Find a current events article about your local municipal government. Narrate your chosen article either orally or written.

Write a Letter

➜ INTRO
Today you are going to write a letter to one of your elected officials. Whether or not you decide to send the letter is up to you. Brainstorm some issues that are important to you, or ask yourself what is one thing you would like to improve about where you live. Do some research about the issue: what needs to change? what has been done about it in the past? Then think about what level of government oversees that area. You can return to the activities from Lesson 25 if you need a refresher. Your local representative for that level of government is the recipient of your letter.

➜ EXAMS

Answer question(s) related to course.
